topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights caught the canvas, illuminating the vibrant chaos of my latest series. It was stunning, undeniably powerful. Then I saw it—a print of my charcoal sketches, subtly altered, hung prominently on a wall I’d only glimpsed from across the room. A cold knot tightened in my stomach. I walked over, my footsteps sounding unnaturally loud on the polished floor. The curator, a woman with an air of untouchable calm, smiled graciously. "Ah, yes, that piece. A wonderful addition to the collection." Her tone was warm, but my jaw felt suddenly tight. I forced a smile, nodding vaguely, my gaze fixed on the slight, almost imperceptible difference in the shading of the figures. It felt like a theft, a quiet appropriation of my very breath.

[story 2]

I stood by the entrance, clutching my lukewarm coffee, watching the crowd mill about. Every glance seemed to snag on the featured exhibition, and there, dominating the main wall, was my work. It was displayed with such confidence, such effortless acceptance. A sharp, unpleasant sensation prickled behind my eyes. I shifted my weight, my fingers flexing involuntarily against the cool ceramic mug. I watched a young patron lean in close, tracing the lines of a piece that was mine, yet somehow belonged to someone else now. A bitter taste rose in my throat. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to smooth the sudden tremor in my hands, determined to appear utterly unbothered, though my chest felt constricted, heavy with unspoken resentment.

[story 3]

The velvet rope felt like a physical barrier separating me from the spectacle. I found myself circling the display, my eyes darting from one piece to the next, landing repeatedly on the one that bore my signature style. The air around that canvas seemed charged, magnetic. When I finally dared to approach, a voice—smooth, assured—explained the provenance of the work. As he spoke, I noticed the way the light played across the texture, a texture I had painstakingly built over months. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck. I stopped breathing for a moment, feeling a strange, hollow ache settle deep within my core. I managed a brittle, polite inquiry, my voice sounding thin and strained, acutely aware of the distance between what I created and what was being celebrated.
